summary:
The chosen answer emphasizes using an ultrasound because it can rapidly diagnose potential urinary tract blockages safely and effectively in critically timed neonatal care scenarios involving no risk factors against its use such as contrast allergies mentioned elsewhere but irrelevant here since we're focusing solely on initial diagnostic methods suitable across all patient types including those with known contraindications avoided via other tests listed.
An ultrasound was selected over alternatives based primarily upon speed, safety profile allowing repeated imaging if needed during acute presentations requiring immediate intervention before irreversible damage occurs; plus lack of ionizing radiation makes this modality particularly appealing when evaluating infants who may have underlying congenital anomalies predisposing them towards renal dysfunction early postnatally necessitating close monitoring throughout hospital stay until full recovery confirmed through follow up studies once stable clinically indicating resolution of any identified pathology leading back toward normal physiology resuming spontaneous voiding patterns consistent with healthy growth trajectory expected within first few weeks after birth completion marking successful treatment course achieved successfully utilizing appropriate technology tailored specifically addressing unique needs associated managing complex medical conditions presenting acutely especially amongst vulnerable populations characterized by limited capacity compensatory mechanisms present at time point evaluated herein suggesting continued vigilance warranted even beyond
